content.problemStatement:
Chef and Divyam are playing a game. They start with a rational number XXX, such that 0<X<10 < X < 10<X<1.

In each turn, Chef chooses a positive real number YYY. Then Divyam either adds or subtracts YYY to XXX. So XXX is replaced with either X+YX+YX+Y or X−YX-YX−Y. If this number becomes 000 or 111, then Chef wins. Otherwise, the game continues.

You are given XXX, and you have to determine whether Chef can win in a finite number of moves. More formally, does there exist a finite number NNN so that Chef has a strategy that is guaranteed to win in at most NNN moves?

Here, XXX will be given in the form AB\frac{A}{B}BA​, where AAA and BBB are positive integers, A<BA < BA<B and gcd⁡(A,B)=1\gcd(A,B)=1gcd(A,B)=1.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers AAA and BBB.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string `"Yes"` if Chef can win the game in a finite number of moves and `"No"` otherwise (without quotes). You can print `"Yes"` or `"No"` in any case.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: Chef will choose Y=12Y=\frac{1}{2}Y=21​, so whatever Divyam does, the number will become 000 or 111. Hence Chef will win in a finite number of moves.
Test case 2: No matter what Chef chooses, it can be shown that Divyam can make sure Chef never wins.
